text: This is this preoperative bronchoscopy which shows an ulcerating fungating lesion in the carina which extends both into the right as well as the left main bronchus.
Surgery begins with a classical posterolateral thoracotomy followed by raising of an intercostal muzzle flap which can be useful later.
We take a pleural incision in the infrazygous region to expose the carina at this juncture.
This is preceded by division of the azygous vein to give us more space to operate in this area.
To expose the carina we need to completely clear off the subcarinal lymph nodal packet.
Doing this not only helps in exposure but if these nodes are involved they can be therapeutic as well.
While performing this step, it is essential to be very careful especially in a post-radiated case as one may injure the airway at an unintended point.
Here we can see a mixture being passed across to create more space in the posterior aspect.
This is followed by looping of the right main bronchus and then followed by looping of the left main bronchus.
Once this is done, we bronchoscopically mark the margins.

accurately just to review the anatomy for our viewers you can see the trachea the right main bronchus the right upper lobe bronchus and the left main bronchus clearly marked in the diagram the resectional part of the surgery begins with division of the right main bronchus it is a usual practice at this point to send these margins for frozen section analysis for ventilation in this patient we have used a cross field ventilation technique with the flexo metallic tube as shown in the video
As the margins were considered to be too close, we had to revise the margin on the right main bronchus.
This is followed by a division of the left main bronchus.
As the left main bronchus is at a depth, it is essential to take a few stage sutures to hold the left main bronchus in place and prevent it from slipping into the mediastinum.

At this point, the saturation started dropping as shown in the picture and we were forced to stop the surgery and re-intubate the patient with a flexometallic tube inserted into the left main bronchus.
but unfortunately patient was not maintaining saturation on that and we had to insert a bronchial blocker as shown in the diagram into the right main bronchus to maintain adequate oxygenation.
Anastomosis begins with formation of the neocarina which involves an anastomosis between the medial part of the right main bronchus and the left main bronchus.
For this anastomosis we have used a continuous running suture technique and the preferred suture material is a PDS 3-0.
The method of reconstruction employed in our case is a Grillo's method of reconstruction which involves resection of the carina followed by formation of a new carina as is being shown in the video.
We had to continuously oxygenate the patient via the bronchial blocker so that we could increase the apnea time and the time available for suturing.
Once the neocarana formation is completed, we go on to form the posterior wall.
This is first preceded by taking stay sutures on the lateral aspect as shown in the video.
Once the stay sutures are taken and the ends are brought closer to each other, we begin the formation of the posterior wall with a continuous suturing technique as described previously.
To maintain ventilation in this patient, it was quite a challenge.
Here, we have used the technique of cross-field ventilation where we use intermittent apnea during which we can do the surgery.
So, as we are seeing in the video now, during this part of the surgery, the patient is not being ventilated and is under complete apnea.
So, this makes it imperative for surgeons to take these sutures quickly but also effectively.
In any airway procedures and especially in a carinal resection, it is essential to have close coordination between the operating teams and the anesthesia teams.
While using a continuous suturing technique, it is imperative to maintain equal spacing and uniform tension across the suture line.
So, it is our usual practice to use a nerve hook to control tighten the sutures as shown in the video.
This ensures good approximation between the cut ends and also ensures a uniform tension across the suture line.
Posterior trifurcation point where the trachea, the right and the left main bronchus meet is a potential area of weakness and these areas need reinforcing as shown in the video.
This is followed by tying of the right lateral stitch which we had taken earlier to approximate the lateral walls together.
Once reconstruction of the posterior wall is complete, we move on to reconstruction of the lateral wall.
It is also done in a similar fashion with a continuous suturing technique.
However, while we were doing this, we were again bothered by the dropping saturation for which we had to re-intubate the patient with the plexometallic tube as shown in the video.
We then move on to reconstruction of the anterior trifurcation point which is marked in the diagram by an arrow.
The anterior wall is reconstructed in a continuous suturing technique as described previously.
The principles of any airway anastomosis are to obtain airtight closure and also to ensure good vascularity of the tissues.
And this is especially important when we are operating in a post-radiated patient.
It might be prudent to use a vascularized tissue flap such as the serratus anterior or the intercostal muscle flap to reinforce the sides of these anastomosis.
Here you can see the final suture being taken and the anastomosis being secured.
This is followed by an air leak test.
Unfortunately in this patient some air leak was noted which was found to be arising from the posterior trifurcation point which had to be reinforced again with a tissue pledged as shown in the video.
After this we reinforced the entire anastomotic area with a vascularized serratus anterior muscle flap and then the chest was closed.
